content.body: Statistics involves collecting, organising, and interpreting data. Measures of central tendency describe the 'typical' value in a data set. Measures of spread describe how spread out the data is.

MEAN (Average):
Mean = Sum of all values ÷ Number of values

MEDIAN (Middle value):
Arrange data in order, find the middle value.
If even number of values, median = average of the two middle values.

MODE:
The value that appears most often.
A data set can have no mode, one mode, or multiple modes.

RANGE (Spread):
Range = Highest value − Lowest value

content.title: Finding Averages
content.body:
Data: 3, 7, 5, 9, 5, 8, 4, 6, 5

Mean = (3+7+5+9+5+8+4+6+5)/9 = 52/9 = 5.78

Ordered: 3, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9
Median = 5 (5th value in 9 values)

Mode = 5 (appears 3 times)

Range = 9 − 3 = 6

content.title: Even Number of Values
content.body:
Data: 12, 15, 8, 20, 14, 18
Ordered: 8, 12, 14, 15, 18, 20
Median = (14+15)/2 = 14.5 (average of 3rd and 4th)
Mean = (8+12+14+15+18+20)/6 = 87/6 = 14.5
Mode = none (all values appear once)
Range = 20 − 8 = 12

When to use each average:
Mean: best when data is symmetric with no extreme outliers
Median: best when data has outliers (e.g. income data where a few very rich people skew the mean)
Mode: best for categorical data (e.g. most popular shoe size)

A very high or low value (outlier) greatly affects the mean but NOT the median.

questions.question: Ages: 12, 13, 12, 14, 12, 15. Find the mode.
questions.explanation: 12 appears 3 times — more than any other value. Mode = 12.

questions.question: Why is median preferred over mean for income data?
options: Median is not affected by extreme outliers
questions.answer: Median is not affected by extreme outliers
questions.explanation: A few very high earners raise the mean significantly, making it misleading. The median better represents the 'typical' income.

questions.question: Data set has no repeated values. Its mode is:
questions.explanation: If no value appears more than once, the data set has no mode.

questions.question: Mean of 5 numbers is 8. What is their sum?

content.body: When dealing with large data sets, we organise data into frequency tables. This makes it easier to find averages and spot patterns. Histograms and bar charts let us visualise data.

Frequency Table Terms:
Frequency (f): how many times a value occurs
Relative frequency: f/total
Cumulative frequency: running total of frequencies

Mean from frequency table:
Mean = Σ(x × f) / Σf
(multiply each value by its frequency, sum up, divide by total frequency)

content.title: Mean from Frequency Table
content.body:
Number of goals scored per match:
Goals(x): 0  1  2  3  4
Freq(f):   3  5  6  4  2

Σf = 3+5+6+4+2 = 20 matches
Σ(xf) = (0×3)+(1×5)+(2×6)+(3×4)+(4×2)
     = 0 + 5 + 12 + 12 + 8 = 37
Mean = 37/20 = 1.85 goals per match

content.title: Bar Chart vs Histogram
content.body:
Bar chart: used for DISCRETE or CATEGORICAL data
• Bars have gaps between them
• Height = frequency

Histogram: used for CONTINUOUS data in class intervals
• NO gaps between bars
• Area (not height) represents frequency
• Frequency density = frequency / class width

questions.question: In a frequency table, what does 'f' represent?
questions.explanation: Frequency (f) is the count of how many times each value appears in the data.

questions.question: A histogram differs from a bar chart because:
options: There are no gaps between bars and it shows continuous data
It uses lines instead of bars
questions.answer: There are no gaps between bars and it shows continuous data
questions.explanation: Histograms show continuous data with no gaps — the area of each bar represents frequency.

questions.question: Frequency density = ?
questions.explanation: Frequency density = frequency ÷ class width. Used in histograms when class widths differ.

questions.question: Cumulative frequency is:
options: Running total of all frequencies up to that point
questions.answer: Running total of all frequencies up to that point
questions.explanation: Cumulative frequency adds up all frequencies from the beginning to each point.

questions.question: f: 5, 8, 7, 3, 2. Total = 25. Relative frequency of class with f=8:
In a bar chart for shoe sizes, gaps between bars indicate:
options: Discrete data (shoe sizes are separate values)
questions.answer: Discrete data (shoe sizes are separate values)
questions.explanation: Bar charts with gaps are used for discrete (countable, separate) data like shoe sizes.

questions.question: 50 students' test scores. Modal class is 60-70 with f=18. What fraction scored in this range?
